---
title: Parameter inputs from data
---

Not every analysis starts from `ParameterSet` distributions. Some run at one base-case set of point values, some carry a draw matrix exported from another tool, and some carry a posterior sample with weights. This tutorial covers the three `heval.params` entry points that turn each source into a parameter draw matrix: `single_draw` (with `ParameterSet.at_means`), `read_draws`, and `resample_posterior`. It is the narrated version of [`examples/parameter_inputs.py`](https://github.com/pedroliman/heval/blob/main/examples/parameter_inputs.py). It assumes you have read [bring your own outputs](byo-outputs.qmd), which is the analogue on the outcome side. The rule that governs all three: once the result is a tidy draw matrix with an `iteration` index and numeric columns, `run_psa` does not care where it came from.

## A model to run

Two strategies for a chronic disease, standard care versus a new drug, with cost and QALYs per iteration as simple functions of three parameters.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from heval.models import Outcomes
from heval.params import (
    Beta, Gamma, ParameterSet, read_draws, resample_posterior, single_draw,
)
from heval.run import run_psa

def model(draws: pd.DataFrame) -> Outcomes:
    base_qaly = 8.0
    effect_drug = base_qaly + draws["u_gain"] * draws["p_response"] * 10
    costs = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Standard care": 40_000.0, "New drug": 40_000.0 + draws["c_drug"]},
        index=draws.index,
    )
    effects = pd.DataFrame(
        {"Standard care": base_qaly, "New drug": effect_drug}, index=draws.index,
    )
    return Outcomes.from_wide(costs, effects)

params = ParameterSet(
    {
        "p_response": Beta.from_mean_se(0.35, 0.05),
        "c_drug": Gamma.from_mean_se(12_000, 1_500),
        "u_gain": Beta.from_mean_se(0.12, 0.03),
    },
    correlation={("p_response", "u_gain"): 0.4},
)
```

## Base case: one set of point values

`ParameterSet.at_means` wraps the analytic means as a one-row draw matrix at iteration 0, the deterministic run that sits next to the PSA. It is `single_draw(params.means().to_dict())`; call `single_draw` directly when the point values come from elsewhere.

```{python}
base = params.at_means()
run_psa(model, base).summary().round(2)
```

## A draw matrix from another tool

`read_draws` validates a CSV path or a DataFrame as a draw matrix. It honours an explicit `iteration` column and otherwise assigns a fresh index; a non-numeric column raises rather than reaching the model. Here we write a sampled matrix to CSV to stand in for an external export, then read it back.

```{python}
external = params.sample(2_000, seed=1)
external.to_csv("external_draws.csv")
csv_draws = read_draws("external_draws.csv", iteration="iteration")
run_psa(model, csv_draws).summary().round(2)
```

## A weighted posterior

A calibration or a bootstrap may hand back parameter rows with a weight column. `resample_posterior` draws whole rows with replacement in proportion to the weights, so any correlation in the posterior survives, then drops the weight column. Resampling to an `n` larger than the input adds no information; it only smooths Monte Carlo noise in downstream expectations.

```{python}
grid = params.sample(500, seed=2)
grid["weight"] = np.exp(4.0 * grid["p_response"])  # favour higher response
posterior = resample_posterior(grid, n=2_000, seed=7)
print(f"grid mean p_response {grid['p_response'].mean():.3f}, "
      f"resample mean {posterior['p_response'].mean():.3f}")
run_psa(model, posterior).summary().round(2)
```

The resampled mean of `p_response` sits above the unweighted grid mean, because the weights favour higher values, and the reweighting raises the new drug's expected QALYs.

Next: the [calibration workflow](calibration-workflow.qmd) produces such a posterior with `abc_calibrate` and mixes it with literature draws through `mix_draws`.
